sentences.base_language: Jaehyeok signed up for a cooking class at the culinary school, something he had wanted to do for years.
sentences.target_language: 재혁은 몇 년 동안 하고 싶었던 요리 학원에 드디어 등록했다.
sentences.romanized: Jaehyeok-eun myeot nyeon dong-an hago sipeotdeon yori hagwon-e deudireo deungnokhaeotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-고 싶었던** = modifier form of the past desire: **하고 싶었던** (something one had wanted to do). **-고 싶다** (to want to do) → **-고 싶었다** (wanted to do) → **-고 싶었던** (modifying a noun — the thing one had wanted to do). The **-던** ending marks a past state or habitual action that continued up to a certain point: 먹고 싶었**던** 음식 (a dish I had been wanting to eat).
**드디어** = finally, at last — used when a long-awaited moment has arrived: **드디어** 해냈다 (I finally did it). Compare: **마침내** (finally — more formal/literary, often for narrative climax): **마침내** 전쟁이 끝났다 (the war finally ended). **드디어** is more personal and emotionally warm; **마침내** is more formal and dramatic. Both imply a long wait has ended.

sentences.base_language: When he arrived, the instructor — an experienced chef named Sujeong — welcomed the group warmly.
sentences.target_language: 도착하자, 수정이라는 베테랑 요리사가 수강생들을 따뜻하게 맞이해 주었다.
sentences.romanized: Dochaghaja, Sujeong-iraneun betereang yorisa-ga sugangsaeng-deul-eul ttatteutage maj-ihae jueotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-자** = as soon as, upon: 도착하**자** (as soon as he arrived). **-자** attaches to a verb stem to mean 'the moment that happened, the next thing happened': 문을 열**자** 고양이가 뛰어나왔다 (as soon as I opened the door, the cat ran out). This is more immediate than **-자마자** (as soon as — even more emphatic): 도착하**자마자** 전화했다 (called the very moment of arriving).
**-아/어 주다** = to do something for someone's benefit: 맞이해 **주었다** (welcomed them — as a kind gesture toward the students). **-아/어 주다** marks the action as done for another's benefit or as a favor: 설명해 **주다** (explain for someone), 도와 **주다** (help someone). It adds a nuance of service, consideration, or generosity toward the recipient.

sentences.base_language: She announced that they would make a traditional Korean three-course meal from scratch.
sentences.target_language: 그녀는 오늘 한국 전통 삼첩 식사를 처음부터 직접 만들 것이라고 알렸다.
sentences.romanized: Geunyeo-neun oneul hanguk jeontong samcheop siksa-reul cheoeum-buteo jikjeop mandeul geosirado allyeotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)ㄹ 것이라고** = indirect quotation of a future statement: 만들 **것이라고** 알렸다 (announced that they would make). **-(으)ㄹ 것이다** (future/presumptive) + **-라고** (indirect quote marker): this structure reports what someone said about a future plan. Compare plain indirect quote: 만들겠다**고** 알렸다 (announced that she would make — volitional). The **것이라고** version is more formal and factual.
**처음부터** = from the beginning, from scratch: **처음** (first/beginning) + **부터** (from). **부터** marks the starting point: 처음**부터** 끝까지 (from beginning to end), 오늘**부터** 시작하다 (start from today). Compare: **에서** (from — for physical location): 서울**에서** 왔다 (came from Seoul). **부터** is for temporal or sequential starting points; **에서** for spatial.

sentences.base_language: Jaehyeok had never cooked anything more complex than ramen, so he felt both excited and nervous.
sentences.target_language: 재혁은 라면보다 복잡한 요리를 해 본 적이 없었기에, 설레면서도 긴장이 되었다.
sentences.romanized: Jaehyeok-eun ramyeon-boda bokjaphan yori-reul hae bon jeogi eopseotgie, seollemyeon-seo-do gingjang-i doeeotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-아/어 본 적이 없다** = to have never experienced doing: 해 **본 적이 없었다** (had never done). **-아/어 보다** (to try doing) + **-(으)ㄴ 적이 있다/없다** (have/have not had the experience of): a very common structure for discussing past experience. Compare: **해 본 적이 있다** (have done before), **해 본 적이 없다** (have never done).
**-면서도** = even while, despite the fact that: 설레**면서도** 긴장이 되었다 (felt nervous even while being excited). **-면서** (simultaneous action) + **도** (even/also) creates a concessive simultaneous nuance — two states coexist in mild contrast: 알**면서도** 모른 척했다 (pretended not to know even while knowing). This is distinct from plain **-면서** which is neutral simultaneity.

sentences.base_language: First, Sujeong demonstrated how to hold a knife correctly to avoid cutting their fingers.
sentences.target_language: 먼저, 수정은 손가락을 베지 않도록 칼을 올바르게 잡는 법을 시연해 보였다.
sentences.romanized: Meonjeo, Sujeong-eun sonegarag-eul beji andorok kal-eul olbareuge jabneun beob-eul siyeonhae boyeotda.
literal: so as not to cut fingers
sentences.grammarNote:
**-지 않도록** = so as not to, in order to avoid: 베**지 않도록** (so as not to cut). **-도록** expresses purpose or goal: 늦**지 않도록** 서두르다 (hurry so as not to be late), 이해하**도록** 설명하다 (explain so that they understand). **-지 않도록** combines negation **-지 않다** with the purpose **-도록**: a very practical construction for expressing precaution.
**-(으)는 법** = the way/method of doing: 잡**는 법** (the way to hold). **법** (法, law/method) in this grammatical pattern means 'how to' or 'the correct way': 먹**는 법** (how to eat), 쓰**는 법** (how to write/use). Compare: **방법** (way/method — more formal noun): 요리 **방법** (cooking method). **-(으)는 법** is a natural everyday expression for instructions or technique.

sentences.base_language: She explained that most kitchen accidents happen because people do not concentrate properly.
sentences.target_language: 그녀는 부엌에서 일어나는 사고 대부분이 제대로 집중하지 않아서 생긴다고 설명했다.
sentences.romanized: Geunyeo-neun bueog-eseo ireonaneun sago daebubun-i jedaero jipjunghaji anaseo saenginda-go seolmyonghaetda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-지 않아서** = because of not doing: 집중하**지 않아서** (because they do not concentrate). **-지 않다** (negation) + **-아/어서** (cause/sequence): the **-아/어서** of cause links the reason directly to the result. Note: **-아/어서** for cause cannot be used in imperative or propositive sentences — use **-(으)니까** in those contexts: 늦**으니까** 서둘러라 (hurry because you're late — command).
**생기다** = to arise, to occur, to come into being: 사고가 **생기다** (an accident occurs). **생기다** is the intransitive counterpart of **만들다** (to make): it describes things that happen or appear naturally. Also: 기회가 **생기다** (an opportunity arises), 문제가 **생기다** (a problem comes up). More colloquial than **발생하다** (to occur — formal/written).

sentences.base_language: Jaehyeok practiced chopping onions and his eyes began to water almost immediately.
sentences.target_language: 재혁은 양파를 써는 연습을 했는데, 거의 바로 눈에서 눈물이 나오기 시작했다.
sentences.romanized: Jaehyeok-eun yangpa-reul sseneun yeonseub-eul haetnneunde, geoui baro nun-eseo nunmul-i naogi sijakhaetda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-기 시작하다** = to begin to do, to start doing: 나오**기 시작했다** (began to come out). **-기** nominalizes the verb, and **시작하다** (to begin) takes the nominalized form as its object: 비가 오**기 시작했다** (it started raining), 이해하**기 시작했다** (started to understand). Compare: **-기 시작하다** vs **-(으)려고 하다** (to be about to do — intent, not yet started).
**눈물이 나다** = tears come out, to tear up: 눈**에서** 눈물**이 나다** (tears come from the eyes). **나다** here is an intransitive verb of emergence: 냄새가 **나다** (a smell comes out), 소리가 **나다** (a sound comes out), 눈물이 **나다** (tears come out). This natural-process framing is characteristically Korean — rather than 'my eyes water,' Korean describes tears as emerging on their own.

sentences.base_language: The other students laughed kindly, and Sujeong said it meant the onions were very fresh.
sentences.target_language: 다른 수강생들은 친절하게 웃었고, 수정은 그건 양파가 아주 신선하다는 뜻이라고 말했다.
sentences.romanized: Dareun sugangsaeng-deul-eun chinjeolhage useotgo, Sujeong-eun geugeon yangpa-ga aju sinseonal-daneun tteusirado malhaetda.
literal: that the onion is very fresh (modifier)
sentences.grammarNote:
**-다는 뜻** = the meaning that ~, it means that ~: 신선하**다는 뜻** (the meaning that it is fresh). **-다는** (indirect speech modifier) + **뜻** (meaning/implication): used to explain what something signifies. Compare: **-다는 말** (the word/statement that ~), **-다는 뜻** (the meaning/implication that ~). This construction is very useful when interpreting a sign, gesture, or situation: 그게 무슨 **뜻**이에요? (What does that mean?)
**-이라고 말하다** = to say that ~ is: 뜻**이라고** 말했다 (said that it was the meaning). **-이라고** is the indirect quotation form of the copula **이다** (to be). Quote pattern: **noun + 이라고** = 'said it was [noun].' Compare: **verb + -다고 말하다** (said that [verb happened]), **adjective + -다고 말하다** (said that [adjective is true]), **noun + 이라고 말하다** (said it is [noun]).

sentences.base_language: For the main course, they prepared a slow-simmered Korean stew with seasonal vegetables and tender beef.
sentences.target_language: 주요리로는 제철 채소와 부드러운 쇠고기를 넣고 천천히 끓인 한국식 찜을 만들었다.
sentences.romanized: Juyori-ro-neun jecheorl chaeso-wa budeureorun soegogi-reul neoko cheoncheonhi kkeurin hanguksik jjim-eul mandeureotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**제철** (jecheorl) = in season, seasonal: **제철** 채소 (seasonal vegetables), **제철** 과일 (seasonal fruit). **제** (proper/correct) + **철** (season/time): the right season for something. **제철에 나는 것이 맛있다** (things in season taste the best) — a common Korean expression about appreciating seasonal food. Compare: **계절** (gyejeol, season — general), **철** (cheol, season in an agricultural/cyclical sense).
**-(으)ㄴ** past modifier with action verbs: 천천히 끓**인** 찜 (a stew that was slowly simmered). The **-(으)ㄴ** modifier on an action verb indicates a completed action modifying a noun: 구**운** 고기 (grilled meat), 삶**은** 달걀 (a boiled egg). This is distinct from the **-는** present modifier (끓이**는** 찜 — a stew being simmered) and **-(으)ㄹ** future modifier.

sentences.base_language: Sujeong walked around, tasting each person's dish and offering quiet suggestions.
sentences.target_language: 수정은 돌아다니며 각자의 음식을 맛보고 조용히 조언을 해 주었다.
sentences.romanized: Sujeong-eun doradanimeyo gakja-eui eumsig-eul matbogo joyonghi joeun-eul hae jueotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-며/-면서** simultaneous action: 돌아다니**며** (while walking around). **-며** is the contracted form of **-면서**, used when two actions happen at the same time by the same subject: 음악을 들으**며** 공부했다 (studied while listening to music). **-며** is slightly more literary; **-면서** is more colloquial. Both require the same subject for both actions.
**각자** (gakja) = each person, each one individually: **각자의** 음식 (each person's dish). **각** (each) + **자** (person): **각자** 먹다 (each person eat on their own), **각자** 생각이 다르다 (everyone has different thoughts). Compare: **각각** (each one — can modify things as well as people): **각각** 다르다 (each is different). **각자** specifically refers to individual people.

sentences.base_language: Jaehyeok accidentally put in too much salt but Sujeong told him it was an easy mistake to fix.
sentences.target_language: 재혁은 실수로 소금을 너무 많이 넣었지만, 수정은 쉽게 고칠 수 있는 실수라고 했다.
sentences.romanized: Jaehyeok-eun silsu-ro sogeum-eul neomu mani neokeotjiman, Sujeong-eun swipge gochil su inneun silsurago haetda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)ㄹ 수 있다** = can, to be able to: 고칠 **수 있는** 실수 (a mistake that can be fixed). **-(으)ㄹ 수 있다** expresses potential or possibility: 할 **수 있다** (can do), 먹을 **수 있다** (can eat). The modifier form **-(으)ㄹ 수 있는** creates a relative clause: 먹을 **수 있는** 것 (something edible), 고칠 **수 있는** 실수 (a fixable mistake). Compare: **-(으)ㄹ 수 없다** (cannot, impossible).
**실수로** = by mistake, accidentally: **실수** (mistake) + **로** (by means of). **-(으)로** marking cause/manner: 실수**로** 넣다 (put in by mistake), 실수**로** 전화했다 (called by mistake). Compare: **우연히** (by coincidence — not necessarily an error), **잘못하여** (by doing wrong — more formal). **실수로** is the most natural everyday expression for accidental actions.

sentences.base_language: She showed him how to balance the flavor by adding a bit of vinegar and a little sugar.
sentences.target_language: 그녀는 식초를 조금과 설탕을 약간 넣어 맛의 균형을 맞추는 방법을 알려 주었다.
sentences.romanized: Geunyeo-neun sikcho-reul jogeum-gwa seoltang-eul yakgan neoeo mas-eui gyunhyeong-eul matchuneun bangbeob-eul allyeo jueotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**알려 주다** = to inform/teach someone, to let someone know: 방법을 **알려 주었다** (showed him the method). **알리다** (to inform, to let know — causative of 알다) + **-아/어 주다** (for someone's benefit): a doubly benefactive structure. Compare: **가르쳐 주다** (teach — more skill-based), **알려 주다** (let know/inform — more information-based). Both are common in teaching contexts.
**균형을 맞추다** = to balance, to bring into balance: 맛의 **균형을 맞추다** (balance the flavor). **균형** (均衡, balance/equilibrium) + **맞추다** (to match/align): a natural collocation. Compare: **균형이 잡히다** (balance is achieved — passive/resultant): 균형이 잡힌 식단 (a balanced diet). **맞추다** implies active effort to align things; **잡히다** implies the balance settling into place.

sentences.base_language: The aroma from the pot filled the kitchen and made everyone's mouth water.
sentences.target_language: 냄비에서 올라오는 향기가 부엌을 가득 채우며 모두의 입맛을 자극했다.
sentences.romanized: Naembi-eseo ollaoneun hyanggi-ga bueog-eul gadeuk chaeumeyo modu-eui ibmas-eul jageukhaeotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**올라오다** = to rise up, to come up: 냄비에서 **올라오는** 향기 (the aroma rising from the pot). **올라** (going up) + **오다** (coming toward): a directional compound verb. Compare: **올라가다** (to go up — away from speaker), **올라오다** (to come up — toward speaker). The modifier **올라오는** (present tense) shows the aroma is actively rising as you observe it.
**입맛을 자극하다** = to stimulate the appetite, to make one's mouth water: **입맛** (appetite, literally 'mouth taste') + **자극하다** (to stimulate). **입맛** is a broader concept than hunger: 오늘 **입맛**이 없다 (I have no appetite today), **입맛**이 당기다 (one's mouth waters/cravings are triggered). **자극하다** (刺戟하다, to stimulate/provoke) captures the involuntary sensory response.

sentences.base_language: By the time the food was ready, Jaehyeok's arms were tired from all the stirring.
sentences.target_language: 음식이 완성될 무렵에는, 재혁은 계속 저어서 팔이 다 지쳐 있었다.
sentences.romanized: Eumsig-i wanseongdoel mureob-e-neun, Jaehyeok-eun gyesok jeoeoseo pal-i da jichy-eo isseotda.
literal: by the time the food was ready-TOPIC,
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)ㄹ 무렵에** = around the time when ~ (future/anticipated): 완성될 **무렵에** (around the time it would be ready). **무렵** expresses approximate time and is used with the future modifier **-(으)ㄹ** when looking forward to a time, or the past modifier **-(으)ㄴ** when looking back: 도착했**을 무렵** (around the time of arrival — past). It adds a sense of 'approximately at that point in time.'
**-아/어서** cause with physical result: 계속 저어**서** 팔이 지쳤다 (arms became tired from continuously stirring). **-아/어서** connects an action as the direct cause of a result state. Note the sequential/causal nuance: the continuous stirring caused the tiredness. **계속** (gyesok) = continuously, without stopping — **계속 하다** (keep doing), **계속 저어서** (from stirring on and on).

sentences.base_language: For dessert, they made a simple rice cake dessert that required careful attention to timing.
sentences.target_language: 후식으로는, 시간 조절에 세심한 주의가 필요한 간단한 떡 디저트를 만들었다.
sentences.romanized: Husig-euro-neun, sigan jojeol-e sesimhan juui-ga piryohan gandanhan tteok dijeo-teu-reul mandeureotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)ㄴ** modifier of adjective/descriptive verb: 필요**한** (required/necessary — modifying 디저트). Descriptive verbs (adjectives) use **-(으)ㄴ** to modify nouns in all tenses: 간단**한** 요리 (simple cooking), 세심**한** 주의 (careful attention), 필요**한** 과정 (required process). This contrasts with action verbs which use **-는** for present and **-(으)ㄴ** for past.
**세심하다** (sesimhada) = to be careful, meticulous, attentive to detail: **세심한** 주의 (careful attention), **세심하게** 살피다 (observe with great care). **세** (細, fine/detailed) + **심** (心, heart/mind): a fine-grained attentiveness. Compare: **꼼꼼하다** (thorough, meticulous — checking everything carefully), **신중하다** (careful, prudent — in decision-making). **세심하다** emphasizes noticing small details; **꼼꼼하다** emphasizes not missing anything.

sentences.base_language: Jaehyeok felt proud when his dessert came out just right and looked beautiful.
sentences.target_language: 자신의 디저트가 딱 맞게 나왔고 보기에도 아름다워서 재혁은 뿌듯함을 느꼈다.
sentences.romanized: Jasin-eui dijeo-teu-ga ttak matke nawatgo bogi-e-do areumdawoseo Jaehyeok-eun ppudeutham-eul neukkkyeotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**뿌듯하다** = to feel proud and fulfilled (from within): **뿌듯함을 느끼다** (feel a sense of fulfillment). **뿌듯하다** is a distinctive Korean emotion — the warm, swelling pride of having achieved something through effort. Compare: **자랑스럽다** (proud — about someone/something, can be said of others), **뿌듯하다** (fulfilled pride — always about one's own achievement or that of someone dear). **뿌듯한 마음** = a heart full of quiet pride.
**보기에** = in terms of appearance, to look at: 보기**에도** 아름답다 (beautiful even in how it looks). **보다** (to see/look) + **-기에** (in terms of doing, from the perspective of doing): **먹기에** 불편하다 (inconvenient to eat), **듣기에** 좋다 (pleasant to listen to). The **도** adds 'also/even': even in appearance, not just in taste.

sentences.base_language: The class sat together to eat the meal they had prepared, feeling a warm sense of accomplishment.
sentences.target_language: 수강생들은 함께 만든 음식을 먹으며 따뜻한 성취감을 느꼈다.
sentences.romanized: Sugangsaeng-deul-eun hamkke mandeun eumsig-eul meogeumeyo ttatteuthan seongchwigam-eul neukkkyeotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**함께 만든** = made together (modifier): **함께** (together) + **만든** (past modifier of 만들다). **-ㄴ/은** past modifier with action verbs turns them into relative clauses: 함께 **만든** 음식 (the food they made together), 혼자 **쓴** 편지 (a letter written alone). The modifier preserves adverbs: **천천히** 끓인 찜 (a stew slowly simmered).
**성취감** (seongchwigam) = sense of accomplishment: **성취** (achievement) + **감** (感, feeling/sense). **-감** is a productive suffix forming emotion/sense nouns: **만족감** (sense of satisfaction), **행복감** (sense of happiness), **불안감** (sense of anxiety), **성취감** (sense of accomplishment). All describe a felt quality of an emotion, slightly more introspective than the bare emotion noun.

sentences.base_language: Jaehyeok discovered that cooking was more satisfying than he had expected.
sentences.target_language: 재혁은 요리가 예상했던 것보다 훨씬 더 보람 있다는 것을 깨달았다.
sentences.romanized: Jaehyeok-eun yori-ga yesanghaetdeon geotboda hwolssin deo boram itdaneun geot-eul kkaedareotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)ㄴ/는다는 것을 깨닫다** = to realize that ~: 보람 있**다는 것을** 깨달았다 (realized that it was rewarding). **-다는 것** nominalizes a statement (what someone says/realizes): the entire clause 'cooking is rewarding' becomes the object of 깨닫다. Compare: **-다는 것을 알다** (know that ~), **-다는 것을 느끼다** (feel/sense that ~).
**보람 있다** = to be rewarding, worthwhile, meaningful: **보람 있는** 일 (rewarding work), **보람을 느끼다** (feel a sense of meaning). **보람** is a distinctly Korean concept — the sense that effort has been meaningful and worth the investment, particularly when it bears fruit. Compare: **의미 있다** (meaningful — more intellectual), **가치 있다** (valuable — more objective), **보람 있다** (rewarding — subjective, emotional sense of worth).

sentences.base_language: Sujeong invited them all to return for the next class, which would focus on regional Korean cuisine.
sentences.target_language: 수정은 다음 수업에는 한국의 향토 음식을 배울 것이라며 모두를 다시 초대했다.
sentences.romanized: Sujeong-eun daeum sueob-e-neun hanguk-eui hyangto eumsig-eul baeul geosiramyeo modu-reul dasi chodaehaetda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)ㄹ 것이라며** = saying that ~ will / quoting a statement while doing something: 배울 **것이라며** 초대했다 (invited them, saying they would learn). **-(으)ㄹ 것이다** (future) + **-라며** (quoted speech with simultaneous action — contracted from **-라고 하면서**). **-며/-면서** on a quote connective shows that speaking and acting happened simultaneously: 이렇게 하면 된다**며** 가르쳐 주었다 (taught them, saying this is how it's done).
**향토 음식** (hyangto eumsig) = regional/local cuisine, hometown food: **향토** (鄕土, home soil/native land) + **음식** (food). **향토** has a warm, nostalgic connotation — food tied to a specific Korean region and its traditions. Compare: **지역 음식** (regional food — neutral, geographical), **향토 음식** (regional food — with cultural/nostalgic warmth), **향토색** (local color/regional character).

sentences.base_language: Jaehyeok went home with a full stomach and a new passion, already planning what he would cook the following weekend.
sentences.target_language: 재혁은 배부르게 먹고, 새로운 열정을 안은 채 집으로 돌아오면서 다음 주말에 무엇을 요리할지 이미 생각하고 있었다.
sentences.romanized: Jaehyeok-eun baebureuge meokgo, saeroun yeoljeong-eul aneun chae jib-euro doraomeyo daeum jumal-e mueos-eul yorihalji imi saenggakhago isseotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)ㄹ지** embedded question = indirect question about what/whether to do: 무엇을 요리할**지** 생각하다 (think about what to cook). **-(으)ㄹ지** embeds a question as a noun clause: 어디 갈**지** 모르다 (don't know where to go), 언제 올**지** 알다 (know when they'll come). Compare: **-(으)ㄴ/는지** (whether/what — for facts): 어디 있**는지** 몰라 (don't know where it is — present fact).
**-(으)ㄴ 채** = while in the state of, with ~ still in that state: 열정을 안**은 채** (holding new passion — while in that state). **-(으)ㄴ** past modifier + **채** (state/condition): describes carrying a condition into another action. Compare: **-고 있다** (ongoing action), **-(으)ㄴ 채** (maintaining a resulting state while doing something else): 신발을 신**은 채** 들어왔다 (came in with shoes still on).
